data.title: Broadcom: Margin Concerns Are Overblown
data.description:
In late September, I upgraded Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) from a hold to a buy rating as there were strong indications that AI was successfully driving growth for the company. While the valuation was quite high, the fundamentals and bright prospects were enough to justify a bullish stance. As shown below, Broadcom has experienced a bit of a rocky period in past months, but that didn't stop the stock from advancing ~6% since the publication of my previous article. Today, I'll be providing an update to see whether the bull thesis remains intact.

In the analysis below, it is shown that Broadcom's fundamentals are solid with growth accelerating on the top and bottom line. In terms of the near term outlook, revenue growth is expected to remain strong in Q1 and the market has seemingly overreacted to mild margin pressure moving forward. Furthermore, Meta (META) possibly using Google's (GOOGL) TPUs in 2027 shows the market potential of Broadcom's ASICs and so the long term outlook is very bright currently. Therefore, I view the contraction in the forward P/E ratio as a signal that there is a considerable amount of opportunity in the stock right now.

Trajectory Is Impressive

Above is a slide from their December 2025 company overview presentation. It shows the company's annual revenue from 2005 to the present, and you can clearly see that as the AI revolution started, Broadcom's business trajectory has improved considerably. This indicates very real tailwinds.

Another thing I want to mention here is the fact that most of this growth has been organic. While the company has had a history of major acquisitions, note from the above chart that, with the exception of VMware, there haven't been significant mergers or acquisitions in the past five years or so. Therefore, the company's current growth is organic in the sense that they are focused on innovation and R&D as growth drivers. Putting reliance on deals for growth behind them is an encouraging sign in my view. Enough big picture, let's get into their most recent results.

Growth Rebound Continues

Broadcom's FY2025 Q4 represented a second consecutive quarter of top line growth acceleration. This provides further confidence that an inflection point is indeed in and that growth is truly bouncing back in a meaningful way. The company generated record revenues of $18.015 billion, up 28% YoY. This is contrasted with Q3's 22% top line growth rate, and so momentum has clearly picked up. Broadcom was also able to crush expectations as they beat by a very wide margin of $555.89 million. Therefore, for the company as a whole, Q4 was an outperforming quarter.

While both of their segments saw respectable growth rates, it was semiconductor solutions that clearly led the way with $11.072 billion in revenues and a growth rate of 35%. It was reported that AI semiconductor revenue in particular saw growth of 74% YoY. In the previous quarter, AI growth was reported to be 63%, and so there are good signs momentum is strong. At the end of the day, both of their segments saw a speedup in growth, and so there is broad strength in the company, no pun intended.

Google's Meta Deal

By now it is no secret that Meta is likely planning on using Google's TPUs in their data centers. These chips were designed with the expertise of Broadcom, and that makes the implications potentially far reaching. TPUs have been used internally at Google for years and they helped to reduce reliance on Nvidia (NVDA). This reported deal between Meta and Google would be the first external use of TPUs and it may position Broadcom to be even more of a competitor to Nvidia. As Arjun Kharpal of CNBC writes "if Meta uses the TPUs, it would be big win for Google and potential validation of the technology."

In my view, while the market is likely large enough for multiple winners, this development significantly improves the outlook for Broadcom as the positioning of ASICs in the AI semiconductor space may have fundamentally shifted. Meta will reportedly start using TPUs in 2027, and so the longer term outlook is exciting for Broadcom.

Profitability Is Strong

In their Q4, profitability showed some improvement. For their adjusted EBITDA margin, they reported 68%. In the prior year period, this metric was at 65% and so there has been considerable expansion. Of course, adjusted EBITDA margin is usually a great indicator of a company's true business efficiency and so this is a promising sign. For the bottom line, Broadcom reported an adjusted EPS of $1.95, up 37%. As shown above, this is a mild acceleration from the previous quarter and note that it also outpaced the top line growth rate as a sign of robust profitability. In just a minute, we'll discuss some potential margin concerns moving forward but the chart above shows that consensus expectations call for strong EPS growth in Q3 and Q4 of FY2026.

Dividend Has Been Raised

In addition to solid profitability in Q4, cash flows remained robust. Despite both operating cash flow growth and FCF growth slowing a bit from the previous quarter, both growth rates are largely in-line with bottom line growth and significantly above the top line growth rate. Therefore, Broadcom's cash generation has been at very respectable levels.

In terms of stock buybacks, there weren't any for Q4 nor the prior year period. However, for FY2026 as a whole, repurchases were down sharply from $7.176 billion to $2.45 billion. Therefore, there seems to be some hesitancy to return capital. That said, for Q4, dividend payout increased from $2.484 billion to $2.797 billion YoY and for the full year, payout rose from $9.814 billion to $11.142 billion. In addition, it was announced that the company will increase FY2026 quarterly dividends by 10% to $0.65, and so while overall capital returns may be down due to fewer buybacks, income seekers may be happy with what they see.

Margin Concerns Overblown

For FY2026 Q1, Broadcom is expecting to see revenues of around $19.1 billion. This was above analyst expectations of $18.38 billion and represents a growth rate of 28% YoY. This growth would basically be unchanged from Q4. While further growth acceleration would have been more desirable, this projected Q1 increase in revenue still indicates that business activity will remain robust.

As for adjusted EBITDA margin, they are expecting it to be at 67%. This would be a minor contraction from the year ago quarter's 68%. In their earnings call, CFO Kirsten Spears stated that "we expect Q1 consolidated gross margin to be down approximately 100 basis points sequentially, primarily reflecting a higher mix of AI revenue." In my view this really isn't that big of a surprise as a higher percentage of hardware revenue contribution and a lower percentage of software revenue contribution intuitively leads to some margin pressure. In my view, the market likely overreacted and that may present an opportunity.

A Buying Opportunity

Despite the stock having advanced since my previous article, the forward P/E ratio has contracted sharply, with Broadcom having received 42 FY1 up revisions in the past 90 days versus zero FY1 down revisions. In my view, the valuation shows that there is a good deal of opportunity right now.

Taking a step back, Broadcom showed that their AI business in particular and their business as a whole saw notable acceleration. In the guidance discussed above, they are signaling that strength will continue, and in my view, margin concerns are significantly overblown. Furthermore, this near term guidance simply does not capture the long term opportunity at hand. With Meta reportedly starting to use Google's TPUs starting in 2027, Broadcom's ASICs could become a major competitor to Nvidia's GPUs, and so the best is likely yet to come for the company.

With that in mind, I view the contraction in the forward P/E ratio as a good indication that the risk/reward is firmly in the bulls' favor and that should result in outperformance moving forward.

Conclusion

At this point in time, there are no indications that business is slowing down for Broadcom. On the contrary, an inflection is now confirmed to be in, and guidance for Q1 looks solid in my eyes. Furthermore, investors should be more focused on long term opportunities like external TPU sales rather than a mere 100 basis points of potential gross margin contraction. The forward P/E ratio's recent drop is an indication that the market has grown indifferent to Broadcom's increasingly bright prospects, and that creates considerable opportunity. As a result, I believe a reiteration of my buy rating is justified at this juncture.

data.title: United Community Banks Is Inching Toward An Upgrade
data.description:
January 14th was a pretty good day for shareholders of United Community Banks, Inc. (UCB). Shares of the company rose 3.3% after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Although earnings per share fell short of analysts’ expectations, revenue exceeded forecasts. The company demonstrated continued growth, not only when it came to the balance sheet, but also when it came to the income statement. Asset quality is looking decent, and relative to other comparable firms, the institution is not what I would call expensive.

In the past, I have taken a more neutral stance on the business. In March of last year, I decided to upgrade the stock from a Sell to a Hold. This was thanks to improvements that the company had made, including the firm's acquisition of ANB Holdings and the launch of its United Community Wealth endeavor.

At that time, the firm was not yet attractive enough to justify a bullish outlook. And the share price performance since then has reflected that. The stock is now up only 5.6% since that article was published. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up 17.8%. These recent improvements are great to see. And moving forward, investors should expect more fundamental growth.

However, the stock is just a hair away from being worthy of an upgrade. That's why, even though I am impressed with the fundamental performance of the business, I believe that maintaining it as a Hold candidate is the appropriate choice. However, it wouldn't take much for me to upgrade it from here.

Management Continues To Make Improvements

Pretty much any way that you look at it, the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was a good time for United Community Banks. Take the balance sheet as an example. The company ended the final quarter with deposits of $23.80 billion. That represents a nice increase over the $23.46 billion that the institution had at the end of 2024. What's really impressive is that this growth was the result of almost no material change in brokered deposits. These increased only slightly from $168.3 million to $175.3 million. Even with that rise, however, these high-cost deposits that are borrowed from other financial institutions are basically statistically insignificant.

Long term, we should probably expect deposits to continue expanding. The company is particularly proud of the fact that its footprint has a focus on certain high-growth southeast metropolitan statistical areas. As an example, we have Jacksonville and Orlando, which are both in Florida. Miami and Tampa are also included. Both Raleigh and Charlotte in North Carolina, Nashville in Tennessee, Atlanta, in Georgia, and Greenville in South Carolina, represent some of the most attractive markets for the institution. The company also has a foothold in some of the fastest growing mid-sized metropolitan statistical areas in that region. And what's great here is that this is not just historical growth. It's growth that is likely to continue moving forward.

The management team at United Community Banks expects that the population growth of the top 10 fastest growing major areas in which it operates will range between 3.99% and 8.79% from 2026 through 2031. And with that, we should expect household income to grow by between 9.73% and 14.76%. For the mid-sized areas, growth has been forecasted on the population side to range between 7.10% and 10.89% over the next few years. If for household income, we are looking at expansion of between 8.85% and 16.49%. This should all naturally bode well for the long-term trajectory of the institution.

This kind of expansion is always great to see. And with a rise in deposits typically comes an increase in the size of the loans outstanding. During the quarter, the company had loans of $19.17 billion. This compared favorably to the $17.97 billion the institution had a year earlier. Overwhelmingly, the institution is focused on holding commercial and industrial loans on its books. About 43% of all loans fall under that category. However, the institution also has a lot of exposure to commercial real estate loans and residential mortgages, with those categories accounting for 25% and 16%, respectively, of the entire loan balance that the institution boasts.

Of course, we should be looking at other aspects of the balance sheet also. The company ended the 2025 fiscal year with $5.99 billion in securities on its books. That is down from the $6.80 billion it had a year earlier. At the same time as this, cash and cash equivalents contracted from $519.9 million to $395.8 million. Meanwhile, debt for the institution fell quite nicely, contracting from $449.2 million to $205.4 million.

These changes might seem at odds. But what I see here is a company that is purposely paying down debt that is likely higher interest in terms of cost, while also prioritizing loan growth above any other investment opportunity. The objective here is almost certainly to capture fixed interest rates for extended windows of time now that interest rates are on the decline. A drop in interest rates should ultimately push the value of these loans higher on the balance sheet. And management is looking to benefit from that also.

The income statement has certainly been a beneficiary of the changes that management is making. Net interest income amounted to $224.3 million for the final quarter of 2025. That's up nicely from the $198.9 million reported the previous year. The increase in the size of the company's balance sheet certainly helped. However, that's not the only benefit the company experienced. The change in the composition of its balance sheet pushed net interest margin up from 3.26% to 3.62%. According to the data provided, we did unfortunately see a slight increase in the provision for credit losses from $11.4 million to $13.7 million. But even with that, the overall picture here is positive. And that has pushed net interest income for 2025 in its entirety up to $860.3 million, compared to the $776.4 million the institution reported the year before.

Non-interest income deserves our attention also. But unfortunately, it was pretty uneventful, essentially remaining flat at $40.5 million year over year for the final quarter. But the good news is that, driven largely by changes in gains and losses on the sale of other loans, and to a lesser extent by service charges and fees rising, this metric grew from $124.8 million for all of 2024 to $154 million for all of 2025.

It is important to point out here that when analysts look at revenue, they are calculating net interest income before provisions for credit losses, combined with non-interest income. That metric amounted to $237.9 million for the final quarter, which was up from the $210.3 million reported the previous year. It also happened to be $4.8 million above what analysts had hoped it would be. Earnings per share, on the other hand, came in short of expectations, but only to the tune of $0.01. Context is important here, though. Because even though the company fell short on expectations, the overall rise from $0.61 to $0.70 meant an increase in net profits from $73.7 million to $85.9 million. This pushed overall net profits for the year up to $318.2 million, which was well above the $244.6 million reported a year earlier.

If we use the results that the company achieved for 2025, we would get a price to earnings multiple of 12.4. In the chart above, you can see that this puts the company around the middle of the five institutions that I compared it to, with three of them trading cheaper than it is. That's not bad, but considering that I also typically like to see a price to earnings multiple of no more than 10, the company doesn't exactly scream discount to me.

In the chart below, I then compared it to the same five institutions when it comes to the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. Here, the picture is better, with the firm trading cheaper than all but one of these enterprises. The fact that the price to book multiple is also very close to 1 is great in and of itself.

We should also be paying attention to certain asset quality metrics. According to management, return on assets for the most recent quarter amounted to 1.21%. In the chart below, you can see that this puts the company tied as the second highest of the institutions in that chart. That also happens to be comfortably above the one minimum that I typically like to see.

However, in the subsequent chart, you can see that the return on equity for the institution is a bit lower than I prefer. I typically like to see a reading of 10% or greater. But right now, we are looking at a reading of 9.48%. That actually puts United Community Banks below any of the five institutions that I stacked it up against. So in that regard, the company is a laggard.

One last thing that we should touch on would be credit quality. This is an area where the institution does well enough in, but it's not exceptional. From the end of 2024 to the end of the 2025 fiscal year, we did see a decline in non-performing assets from 0.64% to 0.48%. That does put the company just below the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see. Meanwhile, the past due loan percentage did increase slightly from 0.17% to 0.23%. But that's not a huge change in the grand scheme of things, and it is still quite low. Net charge-offs have increased. And that should be monitored moving forward.

Takeaway

On the whole, I think that United Community Banks is getting better. But it's not yet at a place that I am comfortable turning bullish on. The overall financial health of the enterprise is looking up. But this stock is not yet cheap enough for the quality that investors get in order to excite me all that much. It is encouraging that management said that they expect more share buyback activity for 2026. They also anticipate an emphasis on organic loan growth. But unless we see something really exceptional, or we see a decline in share price, I believe that maintaining the enterprise as a Hold is the right choice.

data.title: Element Solutions: Well-Placed For Eventual End-Market Turns
data.description:
As a general rule, it is good to be a picks-and-shovels provider to growth markets. I believe that’s an apt description for Element Solutions (ESI) and its Electronics specialty chemical business, particularly with ongoing investments into data center capacity and a recovering general semiconductor market. While other markets like autos are still a drag, I don’t see that side of the business getting all that much worse, and management continues to steer resources toward that Electronics business.

ESI shares are up only about 8% since my last update. There aren’t really good broader comps for this stock, as neither industrial nor semiconductor sector indicators are as relevant as I’d like. Ditto for specific comps – MKS (MKSI) competes in some markets, but has soared on stronger memory leverage, and while Entegris (ENTG) is a closer comp (and has underperformed relative to ESI), it too is not a great comp.

I think there’s enough upside with ESI to remain bullish, but a lot depends on the broader economy and particularly consumer electronics and data center growth. I do think markets like autos and construction are likely to improve from here, but ESI remains more leveraged to overall macroeconomic and consumer trends than some investors may want for a data center growth/semiconductor recovery play.

Refining The Business Through M&A And Sales

Management has been pretty clear that they see the best future for the company in the electronics space and that they will use tuck-in deals to augment their portfolio and create additional cross-selling opportunities. To that end, there have been three noteworthy deals since my last update on the company.

To start with, the company completed its sale of MacDermid Graphics Solutions, a manufacturer of flexographic printing plates, early in the year. Flexographic plates aren’t really an attractive growth market for ESI anymore, and the company realized a meaningful gain on sale ($72 million out of a $323 million sale price) after about 12 years of ownership (it was part of the much larger MacDermid deal in 2013).

More recently, the company announced the acquisition of Micromax from Celanese (CE) in October and the acquisition of EFC Gases & Advanced Materials this January. Both support the company’s efforts to pivot toward higher-margin growth opportunities.

Micromax will cost the company $500 million for about $40 million/year in high-margin (40% EBITDA ex-metals) inks, pastes, and ceramic materials used to create electrical connections and circuits, precision resistors, and insulating layers for printed electronics and hybrid circuits. These materials help facilitate miniaturization (packing more circuits onto a given space), reduce assembly steps (directly patterning/printing), and allow for more rugged final products, as these inks and other materials can survive temperatures and operating conditions that would degrade typical PCB laminates.

EFC will cost $360 million at a similar 12x multiple, giving the company a business generating around $30 million at 30%-plus EBITDA margins (versus recent EBITDA margins in the low-20%s). EFC is a bit of a different business, focusing on specialty and rare gases, but a large percentage of its end markets are overlapping, including semiconductor manufacturing, satellite/space, and electrical infrastructure.

There are multiple applications for specialty gases in satellite/space, including electric propulsion (xenon and krypton) and manufacturing/systems testing. Other gases like high-purity propylene and fluorine-based products are used in electronics assembly and semiconductor manufacturing and cleaning. The company also sells gas recovery and reprocessing systems, important for both environmental/safety compliance and cost efficiency with its customers.

All told, ESI has been adding margin-accretive growth businesses that will also expand the company’s addressable markets (and share-of-wallet with existing customers), and doing so at reasonable prices.

Mixed Business Trends Are A Modest Concern

While the Electronics business is the “sizzle” to this story, the impact of weaker results in the Industrial & Specialty segment (to be renamed as “Specialties” with the EFC deal) has to be considered as well.

Revenue rose 5% in Q3’25 in organic terms, with 7% growth in Electronics and no growth in the I&S business. A significant portion of I&S’s revenue comes from the auto end-market, where underlying unit build volumes have been weak and seem likely to remain so for at least a few more quarters – most third-party estimates call for flattish build volumes in 2026, with China a significant factor in whether there’s overall growth.

Other markets like construction and offshore energy factor into the overall I&S business. Construction should improve later in 2026, with offshore energy more of a wildcard – the company has recently seen good growth on the back of competitive wins in production fluids, but this market can be quite volatile.

Results have also been somewhat mixed in the Electronics business, with growth slowing from 10% organic growth in Q1’25 to 5% in Q3’25.

Semiconductor sales (up 5% in Q3) have been impacted recently by weaker power demand (tied to EVs), but I’m cautiously bullish on some growth here as EV comps will get easier and other power applications (like data center power) accelerate. Circuitry has remained strong (up 13% in Q3) on strong AI/data center demand and smartphone pre-build, and I expect AI/datacenter strength to continue. Assembly, up 5% in Q3, should be leveraged to healthy high-performance computing demand, but I have some modest concerns about ongoing consumer electronics health (offset at least in part by improving content opportunities for auto and industrial markets).

I also want to briefly discuss some comps. MKS has seen significantly stronger recent growth in its Electronics & Packaging business (up 22% in Q3), but I believe this is being driven in large part by the sale of tools/capital equipment where ESI does not compete and weaker growth (down 2%) in Specialty Industrial.

Meanwhile, Entegris reported 0.5% growth in its Materials Solutions business, while former DuPont (DD) segment Qnity (Q) business saw 7% growth ex-calendar effects. While MKS is a large outlier, I think ESI is doing fine on a relative basis.

Summing it up, ESI basically needs to see ongoing growth in data center capex and a turnaround in the broader semiconductor market. While signs of industrial end-market recoveries are tentative, there does appear to be recovering demand for automation and electrical equipment (which should drive chip demand), and auto chip demand should be helped by lower inventories and rising content (especially in ADAS) even despite soft or weak underlying volumes.

That leaves consumer electronics as more of an unknown for me – I don’t expect this next iPhone cycle to be anything special, and consumer demand for electronics (wearables, peripherals, et al.) is likely to be tied to overall consumer confidence and disposable income.

The Outlook

I don’t expect a big deviation from expectations with fourth-quarter results, as management usually tailors guidance pretty well. Underlying strength in data center spending should remain in place, and I could see some modest uplift from improving auto and industrial end markets, perhaps offset by a little softness in consumer electronics.

As far as guidance goes, I would expect to hear about ongoing strength in data center spending (perhaps with some discussion of opportunities/momentum in power and thermal materials), sluggishness in industrial markets, and a general recovery in semiconductors, with opportunities in particular areas like advanced packaging.

Modeling 2026 is a little challenging right now because of uncertainties about the timing of those two deals. I believe high-teens reported growth is plausible, with around a 23% EBITDA margin. Beyond this, I expect high single-digit revenue growth for the next five years and long-term annualized growth close to 7% on the ongoing opportunities in the Electronics business.

I expect margins to improve to the mid-20%s, and I could see upside if semiconductor market demand really picks up, as there should be good operating leverage there. That, in turn, should drive mid-teens free cash flow margins and close to double-digit long-term FCF growth.

Both discounted cash flow and margin/return-driven multiples suggest undervaluation, with DCF getting me to around $29/share and a 13.5x forward multiple (discounted to reflect ongoing auto/industrial weakness) supporting a fair value near $34. If the market really heats up again, that multiple could expand into the mid-teens considering what the market has done with this space in the past.

The Bottom Line

I do see some vulnerabilities in the auto, construction, and consumer electronics exposures, but that is at least partly offset by the opportunities to leverage a semiconductor recovery cycle and ongoing growth in data center spending. Moreover, I like the company’s capital allocation strategy and believe it will add value over time. This is perhaps not the most exciting stock for leveraging data center and semiconductor opportunities, but I do think the risk/reward looks pretty respectable now.

data.title: My Ultimate Buy-The-Dip Picks For 10-12% Income
data.description:
I really like buying high-quality, high-yielding stocks because they do not have to generate much in the way of growth to still deliver attractive total returns over time. Additionally, if they are relatively high quality in nature, that reduces the risk that they are going to end up being yield traps that lead to ever-declining principal and income, and ultimately deliver very underwhelming or even terrible long-term total return performance. As a result, whenever a relatively high-quality, high-yielding stock goes on sale for a deep discount while the underlying fundamentals and balance sheet remain healthy, I view it as a very attractive buying opportunity.

With that in mind, today’s article, I am going to discuss two stocks that yield between 10% to 12% right now that have dipped significantly recently, despite maintaining quality underlying fundamentals that I think are attractive buys on the dip.

A Deeply Discounted 10% Yield With Balance Sheet Firepower

The first opportunity I am going to discuss today is Blue Owl Technology Finance (OTF). OTF recently went public last year, so it has a fairly short history in the public markets. That being said, it has a longer track record as a privately held business development company (BIZD), during which time it has done an excellent job of growing its net asset value per share.

This is especially attractive for business development companies since OTF, like many others, invests primarily in senior secured loans, which means underlying capital appreciation is not a common feature, especially given the fairly significant amount by which OTF has grown its underlying NAV per share. OTF invests about 80% of its portfolio in senior secured loans and expects to increase that to about 85% over time, with the remainder in joint ventures, preferred equity, subordinate debt, and common equity investments, which is where it is getting its capital appreciation from. Its portfolio is also well-diversified across 185 companies.

Meanwhile, OTF today trades at a near 20% discount to its net asset value, which makes it look very attractive, especially given its track record of growing its NAV per share over time. Additionally, its non-accrual rate is de minimis, with it sitting at less than 0.1% basis points of its portfolio at fair value, and its average annual net loss rate is just 13 basis points with 16 basis points of net gains since inception, which means that its underwriting track record has been exceptional over time.

Yes, it does have high PIK, but this is an intentional and structured feature at the origination of its investments in order to give its growth-oriented software counterparties greater capital flexibility in fueling their growth, while helping OTF to generate higher returns from their investments.

While I normally would be suspicious of such widespread use of PIK, given the phenomenal underwriting track record that OTF has and its ability to not only preserve but grow NAV over time with low non-accruals, it gives me confidence in buying management’s narrative on this aspect of their underwriting.

Additionally, OTF has a very strong balance sheet with just a 0.57 times net debt to equity ratio. This is well below its upper target limit of 1.25 times, which means that it has enormous flexibility to add incremental leverage to boost net investment income per share over time. Additionally, it has a buyback program, and given the steep discount to NAV in its stock price, it can also buy back stock to increase leverage while also engaging in highly accretive NAV per share transactions. In the third quarter, OTF repurchased stock and is likely continuing to do so at these levels.

The company also has $3.5 billion of liquidity with a five-year weighted average debt maturity term, which gives its balance sheet flexibility to continue investing opportunistically and even aggressively to boost NAV and net investment income per share. This also helps to alleviate concerns with the fact that net investment income per share is slightly below its base dividend per share per quarter. However, the company has significant spillover income that it is using to support its current dividend while it carefully allocates its excess balance sheet capacity to add leverage gradually to increase its return on equity to eventually cover its dividend.

Given how much balance sheet flexibility it has, with its leverage ratio not even half of its upper limit, I am confident OTF will be able to add the incremental cents per share of net investment income moving forward to fully cover its dividend, even in the face of potential additional Federal Reserve rate cuts.

Another big potential concern for OTF is that its emphasis on software could make it exposed to AI disruption. However, management emphasized on its recent earnings call that the portfolio is well positioned to weather this sort of disruption, given its focus on mission-critical recurring revenue loans to well-established enterprise software businesses that come with high switching costs. Additionally, the relatively short duration and senior-in-the-capital-stack nature of the loans with a 33% average loan to value means that they are insulated even from substantial impairment to software companies if they do begin to get disrupted by AI-fueled competition. They can also see problems coming and exit the investment, potentially even before the loans mature before these problems become too disruptive.

While no investment is risk-free, OTF looks like a very attractive risk-adjusted buy on the dip right now.

A Defensive 12% Yield The Market Is Ignoring

The other opportunity to discuss is Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL). MSDL is more of a traditional BDC and a very defensively positioned one, in that it is more diversified by the segments that it invests in, though it does have substantial exposure to software as well. It is also broadly diversified across 218 individual borrowers.

MSDL today trades at a near 20% discount as well, despite having very low non-accruals at just 1.2% at cost, while its watch list is fairly small, especially compared to many of its peers.

It also has among the very lowest levels of PIK in the industry only 4.1% of its income coming from PIK. It also has the vast majority of its portfolio invested in senior secured loans, with 96% invested in first lien loans. Not only that, but the portfolio is well diversified across 218 borrowers.

On top of that, its leverage ratio is in a good position, with it being right in the middle of its target leverage range, giving it potential additional capacity to add leverage to the balance sheet to further support its investment income per share in the face of interest rate cuts. It has also engaged in creative financing, such as its recent CLO deal at a SOFR plus 1.7% rate, which helped to cut financing costs and further support NII moving forward. Not only that, but it has no debt maturing this year.

MSDL is also managed by Morgan Stanley (MS), which is a high-quality external manager, just as OTF is managed by Blue Owl Capital (OWL), which is also a high-quality external manager, both of which are well aligned with shareholders through considerable equity ownership of these BDCs.

However, despite all these strengths, and a portfolio profile and non-accrual profile that are strikingly similar to peer Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), while its PIK and debt maturity profile are actually more attractive than BXSL’s setup right now, MSDL trades at a huge discount to BXSL, which in my view seems unjustified.

When you combine all of that with its very attractive dividend yield of over 12% today, and the fact that NII per share does cover its dividend per share at a 1.0x level, I think MSDL is extremely attractive from a risk-adjusted perspective. Even if it does end up having to cut its dividend, which is very possible, especially if the Fed cuts rates again this year or cuts rates more than once this year, it will likely be a fairly moderate reduction, or they may switch to a steeper base reduction and then pay an enhanced dividend on top of that, which will likely be quite substantial if the base dividend cut is significant.

MSDL should continue to yield at least 10% on current cost moving forward, while having significant valuation multiple expansion potential due to its steep discount to NAV relative to its strong underlying fundamentals. This is even more the case given that it has one of the most attractive fee structures among externally managed BDCs.

Investor Takeaway

While neither of these investments is risk-free, as concerns about private credit have risen over the past year and there are also concerns about software being disrupted by AI, these are two of the strongest operators in the space in terms of their current fundamentals, their track records, and their external managers. Both also have strong balance sheets with clear value propositions provided by their dividend yields and their steep discounts to net asset value. As a result, I think they are very attractive double-digit yields to buy on the dip and are exactly the type of BDCs that I am looking to add to my 7-8% yielding, low beta, and high-returning portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Eli Lilly: Sell It And Buy Novo Nordisk Instead
data.description:
Introduction

A lot has happened since I rated Eli Lilly (NYSE: LLY) a Hold back in early August, as it appreciated by nearly 42% and reached a $1T market cap, the first in the pharmaceutical industry. With all that has been going on for the company, I believe that the valuation is now overstretched and might peril my Hold rating. LLY is currently the champion in the weight loss industry, but this status could change with the current transition towards the pill war.

Current Dynamics

Eli Lilly saw enormous growth through the injectables Zepbound and Mounjaro, which have been growing at a staggering 40% Y/Y rate. But the company saw a single-day crash of 14% based on the Phase 3 data for orforglipron, as patients lost roughly 11-12% of their body weight, meanwhile, the Street had priced in a 15% loss. Furthermore, high dropout rates due to gastrointestinal side effects led many to fear Novo Nordisk (NVO) would win the oral pill race. See, Novo is holding the first-mover advantage in the space as I highlighted in my piece on the company. The Danish company is currently being highly aggressive with its pricing strategy as it put the starting doses at $149 per month, which is a huge decrease from the $1000 price tag for current injectables. This is a real risk for the company if it cannot match this price for orforglipron, it will lose the massive cash-pay market that Novo is targeting. The injectables market is also not helped by the TrumpRx platform coming online supposedly this month, which will push drugmakers to offer the lowest international prices to US patients.

You might believe that in the end both companies will be impacted the same but I believe that LLY will be impacted more severely due to its market concentration, manufacturing location and the price premium gaps. Lilly relies heavily on the high-margin US market to fuel its entire R&D, especially as 54% of the company’s total revenue was driven by Zepbound and Mounjaro in the country. The mandatory price drop will affect a larger percentage of Lilly’s total valuation pie than it does for Novo. More so, Novo had already begun to lower prices in various markets while Lilly was growing at a 3x faster rate in the US, banking on the super high margins of injectables to justify its $1T market cap. Lilly has already committed over $50B to expand US manufacturing since the turn of the decade, which means that now the company has no choice but to follow US pricing mandates.

Lilly’s pill, orforglipron, is expected to be more convenient as it is a small molecule that can be taken at any time of the day with or without food. It will also be much easier and cheaper to manufacture in massive quantities, though it has higher rates of nausea in the first few weeks. As the pill is set to be released in Q2, Novo will do everything to have a grasp on the market which it can very well do, more so as it is only one part of the strategy. See, the company is also fast-tracking Amycretin which is a dual-action pill that targets both GLP-1 and amylin. The drug is entering Phase 3 trials and is positioned as the Lilly challenger as it can potentially match the weight-loss power of the LLY’s injectables. Though it is likely not set to hit the market until 2028.

LLY also just acquired Ventyx Biosciences for $1.2B, which I view as a highly strategic move in order to own the entire metabolic-inflammation healthcare category. The bid is to buy VTX3232, an oral drug that inhibits NRLP3, that acts as a master switch for inflammation in the body. This will pair well with Zepbound and Mounjaro, which don’t always eliminate the residual inflammation causing heart attacks and liver disease in obese patients.

Valuation

The company’s valuation is for me the biggest risk right now, even if the company’s P/E has decreased from its highs over the past couple of years. LLY has a P/E Non-GAAP of 48.99, which is the highest among its peers, while its FCF yield is among the lowest at 0.9%. This makes LLY vastly more valued as a high-growth tech company compared to a traditional pharma and that’s based on the growth prospects of the Mounjaro/Zepbound franchise, which is projected to reach $60B in sales by 2030. But that is actively priced to perfection, and with the competition being fiercer this year, I do not see how the company will be able to keep these metrics. This dynamic can be seen with NVO’s valuation being a fraction of Eli Lilly’s, while in my opinion, having the best valuation to growth proposition between the two.

The growth prospects for the company are immense, but I will be harsh in evaluating these, as LLY is priced as perfection. LLY could have a concentration cliff as the majority of revenue is derived from a single class of drugs. But if a competitor like Novo or even Viking or Roche takes a large amount of market share away from LLY, the company would have very little non-obesity revenue to catch the fall. Both the Oncology and immunology segments are solid, but they would certainly not be able to offset a major hit to the metabolic franchise. And though it is several years away, the market is already looking at the end of the decade when the first wave of GLP-1 patents will begin to expire. Lilly will have to bank a lot on pills and other weight-loss drugs in a market that will be more mature, which I expect to hurt sales in the future.

LLY is highly profitable as shown by its high gross of 83.03% and net of 30.99%, though these are slimmer than Novo Nordisk. Based on the current shifts in the industry, LLY will have to go through a profitability pivot, as for example Zepbound will drop its price from $1,000 to an average of $346. Lilly will need to garner a larger share of the market through Medicare and uninsured patients to continue the growth trajectory of its earnings.

Conclusion

Right now, if an investor wants to enter the highly lucrative weight-loss industry, I would not recommend buying Eli Lilly shares based on the over-inflated metrics at a time when the company is losing its grip on what is making its stock so expensive. I believe that Novo can catch up and thus the valuation should then come back down to earth. I downgrade my rating of Eli Lilly to a Sell.

data.title: United Community Banks: Set To Break Out
data.description:
We pick up our first day of regional bank Q4 earnings in this column with United Community Banks, Inc. (UCB). This is another small banking network, and this one operates out of the Southeastern region of the United States. The stock has had a nice rebound from April of 2025. However, the stock is about flat from a year ago.

That said, this stock we view as more of a Buy in the space due to its relatively strong dividend history, with consistent annual hikes and a yield of well over 3%. In this column, we examine the critical metrics of this regional bank, which we believe show there is little concern over consumer and business weakness. The stock is flirting with a one-year high and looks set to break out if it can bust through that one-year high resistance.

Headline Results For United Community Banks

In the most recent quarter, United Community Banks, Inc.'s headline results modestly surpassed expectations. Thanks to continued loan growth, United Community Banks saw revenues continue to improve once again. With Q4's revenues of $278.4 million, the company registered a 16% increase in this key metric year-over-year. The result was a beat versus expectations of $5 million. Margins remain in good shape, though a boost to provisions for credit losses offset earnings power.

Margins And Income Power

The increase in revenues year-over-year was once again led by loan growth, though deposits were down sequentially. Margins improved nicely from a year ago and sequentially. United Community Banks saw Q4 net income of $86.5 million, or $0.71 as adjusted per share, for the quarter. This was in line. United Community Banks saw its net interest margin increase by 36 basis points to 3.62% from a year ago, and up 4 basis points from the sequential quarter. This margin increase stemmed from a better loan mix as well as a lower cost of funds.

Loans Grew, Deposits Dipped

We always watch loans and deposits as key metrics. Q4 annualized loan growth was 4.4% from Q3. Loan production was up $209 million. Total loans grew to $19.4 billion, up from $19.2 billion to start the quarter. This is very strong growth in this climate. However, we did see a tick lower in deposits. United Community Banks' deposit base fell $242 million from Q3 to $23.8 billion. Overall this was a bit mixed, but the loan growth was notable, and many deposits remain that allow for future loan growth. Deposits are up about $340 million from the start of the year overall, however.

Asset Quality Mixed

Overall, this bank enjoys strong asset quality, but we saw some deterioration in key metrics in Q4. We noted in the opening that the provision for credit losses ticked up, weighing on earnings. The provisions were up to $13.7 million in Q4, up from $$7.9 million to start the quarter. What about non-performing assets? Well, they improved from Q3. They were 0.33% of assets, improving slightly from 0.35% of assets to start the quarter. This was a result of more loans on the books. Net charge-offs ticked up 18 basis points to 0.34% from Q3, however. As such, we view the asset quality trend as mixed, but nothing is overly worrisome.

Return metrics were about flat from Q3, as the efficiency ratio was nearly flat, though up from a year ago. The efficiency ratio was still strong at 54.4% overall, versus 54.3% in Q3. The return on average assets was 1.22%, while the return on average tangible equity was 13.31%, down from 13.56% sequentially, but up from 12.12% last year. Overall, the bank is performing well.

Take-Home

This was a respectable quarter. We see no signs of an economic slowdown or worsening loan demand. Asset quality as a bit mixed, with notable strengths and weaknesses. While there may a few customers that are falling into nonperforming or nonaccrual status, we see no signs of anything more systematic. The company has also launched a buyback of $100 million, which adds to shareholder returns on top of the dividend. The company executed about $44 million in repurchases in 2025 ahead of this new announcement.

The stock is up from the April 2025 lows, but is about to challenge a one-year-high, which if overtaken suggests a new breakout is ahead. We think given the trends, the valuation, and the dividend growth, UCB shares can be bought.

data.title: Gentex: Shifting Model Mix And A Change In Strategy Hit Sentiment
data.description:
When I last wrote about Gentex Corporation (GNTX) in mid-2024, I thought this was a relatively safe haven in a tough auto supplier space, given the company’s success in gaining share with its automatic dimming mirrors and the company’s overall track record. That was a bad call, as the company has since seen meaningful adverse changes in vehicle production mix and chose to make an odd and questionably strategic acquisition that adds meaningful non-auto exposure.

Gentex shares have lost about 30% of their value since that last article, a notably weak performance relative to other auto suppliers like Adient (ADNT), Autoliv (ALV), and Visteon (VC).

At this point, while I see value in Gentex shares, I have more concerns about the real underlying pace of advanced mirror adoption (and the value that consumers see in them), as well as management’s ability or willingness to remain focused on its core business. I can make the argument that Gentex should be near the lows of this cycle and leveraged to benefit from a shift back to production of higher trim models, but those strategy questions are an issue for me.

Q4 Likely Comes Down To Trim Mix

Gentex hasn’t been doing great of late regarding its performance against sell-side expectations, as the last two quarters have seen modest (2% to 3%) revenue misses versus analyst expectations.

A key concern remains the ongoing underperformance relative to underlying build rates. Shipments and auto revenue have been lagging underlying build rates, with the third quarter seeing an almost 7% decline in auto revenue against low single-digit underlying build growth, with North American shipments flat versus underlying production growth of 5% (OEM revenue was up 5%) and International shipments down 12% against underlying weighted volume growth of 2% (with EU OEM revenue down 14% and China revenue down 35%).

Although fourth-quarter auto-build rates were better than expected, particularly in North America, those discrepancies in recent quarters lead me to some caution on revenue in the fourth quarter. I feel better about gross margin, though, as the company has been outperforming here (even with the drag from the VOXX deal) on operational synergy and restructuring efforts, even while tariffs have been causing issues.

Net-net, then, I see fourth-quarter results as potentially coming in a little weak on the top line but coming in stronger at the operating income line.

Weaker Vehicle Mix Remains A Risk

A big part of the bull thesis on Gentex has been that the overall market for the company’s electronic auto-dimming mirrors is underpenetrated, with these mirrors making up around 35% or so of the overall market (and Gentex having somewhere in the mid-80%s market share, with others like Forvia (FAURY) and Magna (MGA) also involved to a lesser degree).

Given the safety and convenience offered by these mirrors, as well as the higher-function Full Display Mirrors (or FDMs), which include a display unit for rearview cameras, the idea was that Gentex would continue to benefit from ongoing design-ins on more and more models.

That process has stalled of late. While penetration has generally been improving in North America, Europe has seen a reversal driven by a shift to lower-trim models across multiple OEMs. This is likely largely a response to affordability issues, but it remains to be seen how quickly this will reverse. While auto affordability in the EU has improved back to pre-pandemic levels, consumers are still looking to economize, and the outlook for weak production trends in 2026 (most projections are for flat to slightly down build volumes in 2026) doesn’t bode particularly well.

China, too, remains pressured. While I don’t believe trading down in trim levels has been as significant, the market is slowing there as well, and down-trading could become a bigger issue. Moreover, tariffs and counter-tariffs have hurt the business in a meaningful way, with management highlighting this as a main driver in the weak results. Given that China has been one of the only major markets offering underlying unit growth, this remains an ongoing threat.

I’m still basically bullish on the overall adoption trend for auto-dimming mirrors, including the more advanced FDM offerings. While camera systems have gotten some attention, I think adoption will be slower than bulls expect given ongoing safety issues – when Gentex’s mirrors fail, they’re still mirrors, but when a pure camera/monitor system fails, there is no fallback (though some systems include redundant/fallback cameras). Still, as these are premium offerings, the pace of adoption is very clearly sensitive to affordability and varying consumer confidence.

How Big Of A Shift Does The VOXX Deal Represent?

The most significant change in Gentex since my last update is the acquisition of VOXX for about $200 million. VOXX has a presence in the auto supplier market through products like in-vehicle entertainment (especially rear seat entertainment systems), security, and remote start, but about 70% of the business is in consumer audio, including premium brands like Klipsch and Onkyo.

VOXX has not been doing well in the post-pandemic world, with sales falling from around $636 million in FY’22 to an annualized rate of $340 million in Gentex’s third quarter, and Gentex paid only about 0.4x sales to get the company, and I’d note that VOXX itself paid $166 million for Klipsch back in 2011.

It’s not as if this is Gentex’s first foray outside its core mirror business – businesses like HomeLink (vehicle-to-home communications systems) have been in the mix for a while, and the company acquired eSight (electronic vision enhancement technology for vision impairment) back in 2024.

Even so, I have mixed feelings about the deal. On the positive side, enhanced in-vehicle entertainment and infotainment offerings do logically fit with the company’s current strategy of value-added interior components. I can see VOXX offering some solid core technology here, and I can likewise see how security and remote start fit with the core HomeLink technology.

On the other hand, about 70% of this business is in consumer audio – a business where Gentex has no prior experience or core competencies, and where there is clearly a need to restructure and turn around the business to reignite growth and margin leverage. I suppose it is possible to just look at the consumer business as a “call option,” but valuing VOXX only on the directly relevant auto operations does mean that Gentex overpaid for a business (around 1.4x sales) that needs some improvement and doesn’t offer the sort of market share and moats that Gentex’s existing auto business has.

All in all, my biggest concern here is that VOXX’s non-auto businesses become a dilutive, value-destroying distraction for management that they end up selling later at an even lower multiple. There’s turnaround potential here, for sure, but consumer audio is a difficult market, and I need to be convinced that Gentex can turn around this business without it distracting from their growth opportunities in the auto-market.

The Outlook

Gentex has been underperforming my prior expectations for revenue and margins, and the extra margin dilution from VOXX certainly doesn’t help, nor does the outlook for another challenging year in the light vehicle market.

I’m expecting mid-single-digit revenue growth in 2026, and that could yet be optimistic if there isn’t an improvement in trim mix among EU OEMs. I’m looking for a similar 5% revenue growth rate across the next five years and a long-term annualized growth rate of around 4.5%, as I do still see growth opportunities in the company’s mirror offerings and ancillary products (like auto-dimming sunroofs and so on).

On the margin side, I think it will take some time to overcome the dilution from VOXX, and I’m expecting EBITDA margins of around 22% in FY’26 (versus recent years in the mid-20%’s and pre-pandemic margins in the low-30%’s). It’s possible that margin improvement could beat my expectations if the company can successfully renegotiate with OEMs, but given what has happened of late in terms of mix shift, it may be hard for the company to argue that their products are a must-have worthy of higher margins.

I’m expecting low-to-mid-20%s in EBITDA margin to translate into mid-teens free cash flow margins, driving around 6% long-term FCF growth. Again, if the company can do a better job of lifting margins in VOXX and/or secure margins from auto OEMs similar to what they got five or 10 years ago, there’s upside here.

Between discounted cash flow and margin-driven multiples-based valuation, Gentex does look undervalued, with the former giving me a fair value of around $29 and the latter giving me a range of $25 to $28 (with a 1.9x forward revenue multiple and/or a 10x forward EBITDA multiple).

The Bottom Line

There are still things to like about Gentex, including its strong market share in a market that still offers meaningful above-build growth potential, good margins, and solid mid-to-high-teens ROIC. I also like the active share repurchase program and the clean balance sheet.

If management can successfully integrate and improve VOXX, and if consumers go back to demanding vehicles with more bells and whistles, I can see Gentex outperforming. I think the shares are likely to remain relatively limited absent stronger above-build-rate volumes, though, and until there’s more comfort around the VOXX deal. The valuation makes this a name to watch, but I don’t think there’s a need to rush right now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kodiak AI: Competition And Dilution Risk Undermine The Bull Case
data.description:
Kodiak AI, Inc. (KDK) is a developer of autonomous driving technology for trucks that is differentiated by its decision to focus on off-road applications. While it is difficult to compare most self-driving technology companies based on publicly available information, it appears that Kodiak is trailing Aurora Innovation (AUR), and this is reflected in the market capitalizations of the two companies.

The market is still nascent though, and a real leader is yet to be established. As a result, Kodiak's stock could perform well over the next few years if the company can demonstrate the viability of its technology on public roads. This is supported by Kodiak's relatively low cash burn rate and the fact that revenue from off-road operations should start to scale in 2026.

Market

While the adoption of self-driving trucks will eventually be dictated by cost, labor shortages and a potential safety advantage are also reasons to be optimistic about the development of the market in coming years. Human drivers currently cost roughly $1 USD per mile, leading to around $340,000 USD in average annual expenses. Kodiak can eliminate this cost, as well as reducing driver-related overheads and fuel costs.

While the safety aspect still needs to be demonstrated in practice, the fact that over 85% of truck crashes are caused by human error suggests there will be a sizeable advantage long-term. Improved safety will also help transport companies to manage insurance costs, which have risen significantly in recent years. In addition, self-driving trucks facilitate 24 hour operations and will help the roughly 70% of retailers that are targeting next-day or same-day delivery.

Autonomous trucking is an enormous opportunity, even if this isn't really reflected in the market capitalizations of the leading public companies at the moment. The global commercial long-haul trucking TAM alone is over $4 trillion USD, and the opportunity in the U.S. is in excess of $900 billion USD. There is also a $68+ billion USD industrial opportunity and a $150+ billion USD defense opportunity, which is predominately in the U.S.

Kodiak has chosen to initially focus on predominantly off-road opportunities, which could be seen either as an intelligent way of scaling revenue while the technology develops, or an attempt to mask weakness. While I tend to think it is probably a little of each, there is no real reason to believe that the performance of Kodiak's driver won't soon converge with the likes of Aurora.

Self-driving technology could quickly become a commodity if more than a handful of companies introduce viable services. While there is an opportunity for companies to differentiate on cost and safety, I believe that this will be difficulty based on how the market appears to be developing. In addition, there is mounting evidence from other areas that the performance of leading AI models tends to converge over time. A monopoly or a market dominated by 2-3 large players therefore appears unlikely, and as a result, the economics of self-driving technology could be worse than widely anticipated.

Aurora Innovation is one of the better positioned competitors, due to the fact that it has already deployed self-driving trucks on public roads. The company also has a strong balance sheet and relatively large market capitalization, enabling it to invest in growth. While Aurora has driverless trucks on the road, the fact that it isn’t rapidly scaling operations on the Dallas to Houston route suggests this isn’t a solved problem yet.

Kodiak's solution is differentiated by the fact that it does not rely on HD maps, which is critical for off-road operations. Kodiak also hasn't chosen to develop its own sensors, which is an important reason why its cash burn is modest relative to Aurora.

Of note, Aurora plans on commencing autonomous highway operations for Detmar Logistics, hauling proppant between a sand mining site and Detmar's facility in the Permian Basin. Detmar has committed to using 30 Aurora Drivers in 2026, each transporting sand for 20+ hours per day. This could be considered a direct threat to Kodiak, but even within proppant hauling the two companies are pursuing slightly different opportunities.

Plus AI is an American autonomous driving technology company focused on commercial freight trucking. Its PlusDrive solution is initially intended for use with a driver in the vehicle, and can be retrofitted into commercial freight trucks. The company had carried out road testing of its technology in 48 states in the U.S. by the end of 2021. The company also has operations in China, although Plus separated its U.S. and Chinese operations into independent companies in 2023. Plus is partnered with the likes of Bosch and IVECO on the supply side and previously received an order for 1,000 self-driving systems from Amazon. A commercial launch isn't expected until 2027 though. The company plans on going public through a SPAC in the near future, which is expected to raise around $300 million USD at roughly a $1.2 billion USD valuation.

Waabi (private) is another self-driving technology developer that is focused on the freight market. Waabi was founded in 2021 by the former chief scientist of Uber ATG. Its key technologies are the Waabi Driver and the Waabi World simulation platform. Waabi partnered with Uber Freight in 2023, and has regularly been operating between Dallas and Houston since. The company began expanding its operating domain from highways to surface streets in 2024. This is important because more than 40% of the miles traveled by freight trucks are on surface streets. Waabi plans on expanding to other lanes in Texas and beyond, but appears to be behind the likes of Aurora as it only completed fully driverless testing on a closed-course in October.

The relative importance of surface streets suggest that robotaxi companies could successfully adapt their technology to address the freight market. Waymo is perhaps the best positioned candidate to do this, although its trucking initiative was paused in 2023. The decision appears to have been driven by a desire to limit cash burn and the fact that Waymo's robotaxi business had matured to the point that it was ready to scale rapidly. I expect Waymo to restart its freight business in the next few years, and it should grow quickly when it does.

There has also been talk of an autonomous Tesla (TSLA) truck since the Tesla Semi was first announced in 2017. Consumer deliveries of the Semi started in 2022, and PepsiCo (PEP) now has a fleet of almost 100 vehicles. While the Tesla Semi is now on the road in small numbers, it doesn't feature full self-drive, or FSD, at the moment. Tesla is likely to combine its self-driving technology with the Semi platform in the future. The company's decision not to utilize LiDAR or radar could be more of an issue in trucking, though, due to a combination of vehicle weight and speed.

Kodiak AI

Kodiak AI is a self-driving technology company that was founded in 2018 to pursue the autonomous trucking opportunity. The company began hauling freight in 2019 and is operating out of Dallas. Since 2022, Kodiak has been targeting the defense market, which aligns with its initial focus on off-road applications. While it would be difficult to characterize Kodiak as the market leader, it has been operating fully driverless vehicles for Atlas since December 2024.

Detail on Kodiak’s AV system is limited, but it appears to be broadly similar to the technology being developed by the likes of Aurora. Kodiak makes use of parallel deep neural networks and has a proprietary multimodal AI perception model, Giga FusionNet. In terms of sensors, the company leverages cameras, radar and LiDAR.

Kodiak’s technology can operate at day or night and in a wide range of weather conditions, including rain and dust storms. It is also able to operate without high-definition maps, which should support scaling. Kodiak's modular autonomy hardware kit can be adapted to a wide range of vehicles (Class 8 trucks, F-150s, military vehicles) and its sensor pods are designed to be easy to swap in the field. This could be important in coming years, if and when Kodiak seeks to expand beyond trucking.

The deployment of Kodiak's driver is supported by Vay’s remote assistance technology, which is used to provide human judgement, enhancing safety and expanding Kodiak’s operating envelope. Remote support is important for tasks like launching and landing at customer facilities and edge cases like interpreting law enforcement signals. There is little publicly available information about the intensity of support currently needed for self-driving trucks, but presumably the burden is low relative to robotaxis.

Kodiak's AV system is still under development, with updates both driving performance gains and expanding Kodiak's operating envelope. This includes new software that was released at the start of Q4, reducing the need for remote assistance by 53%. The update also introduced a vision language model that helps Kodiak's driver to manage edge cases, which can be difficult for more traditional perception techniques. Kodiak also added the ability to haul 2 trailers at the same time.

Kodiak introduced an Autonomy Readiness Measure in the third quarter, similar to Aurora. ARM measures the percentage of safety case claims that are materially complete. Kodiak’s ARM is 100% in industrial and 78% in long-haul, indicating the company still has significant work to do before it launches commercial long-haul operations. This primarily relates to validation and testing of the system. Even though there is still significant work remaining, Kodiak expects to commence commercial long-haul operations in the second half of 2026.

The impact of Kodiak's system on safety has also been independently assessed by Nauto, a fleet safety technology company. Kodiak received a VERA (Visually Enhanced Risk Assessment) safety score of 98, the highest among 1,000 fleets in Nauto's network (the average VERA Score for a Nauto-equipped fleet is 78). VERA is a proprietary safety benchmark created from over 20 driving variables covering areas like attentiveness, driving behavior and traffic violations. VERA scores range from 1 to 100, with 100 being the safest. Data shows that every 10-point reduction in the VERA score is associated with a 21% reduction in collision rate.

While Kodiak doesn't have fully driverless trucks on public roads yet, it has been conducting operations across 23,000 miles of autonomous lanes. This includes operations between Dallas and Atlanta for J.B. Hunt (JBHT) (over 295,000 miles traveled), and operations between Dallas and Jacksonville and Dallas and Atlanta for Werner (roughly 300,000 miles traveled).

Geographic expansion is supported by a regulatory environment that is becoming increasingly favorable. In particular the Department of Transportation has issued a waiver allowing self-driving trucks to use flashing warning beacons when stopped as a replacement for reflective warning triangles.

Kodiak's ability to scale production of trucks equipped with its self-driving system is likely to come into sharper focus in 2026 due to the Atlas relationship. Contract manufacturers assemble Kodiak’s AV system (sensor pods, compute, ACE) and Roush installs the AV system into OEM vehicles, which enables scale and improves quality relative to in-house production. Roush launched a dedicated manufacturing line in Q3 and the first Roush built driverless vehicles were delivered to Atlas in the quarter.

On the customer front, Kodiak's business is currently dominated by its relationship with Atlas Energy Services. Atlas had 10 operational trucks at the end of Q3 delivering day and night in most weather conditions. The company has also made an initial commitment for 100 trucks, with the larger order coming after Kodiak exceeded key performance and operational milestones in early 2025.

Kodiak has also had some success in the defense vertical, although this work appears to be at the pilot stage so far though. Kodiak entered into a contract with the U.S. Army in 2022 to develop an end-to-end autonomous solution for the Robotic Combat Vehicle Program. Testing has been completed in real-world environments and Kodiak received $30 million USD for the 3-year program. While the Army program was completed in 2025, Kodiak believes that autonomy will eventually be integrated into all defense vehicles and continues to pursue opportunities in this space.

Kodiak is also collaborating with Textron systems in defense, with Kodiak providing autonomous technology for Textron’s vehicles. For example, Kodiak's AV system has been integrated into the RIPSAW M3, which has no room for a driver.

Financial Analysis

While Kodiak's technology is still under development, the company's partnership with Atlas means that revenue is already starting to scale. The company had deployed 10 customer-owned driverless trucks at the end of the third quarter, and has now delivered over 10,000 loads and driven more than 3 million autonomous miles.

Kodiak generated $0.8 million USD revenue in the third quarter, a 53% sequential increase, with growth driven by DaaS revenue. The company expected to end 2025 with a mid to high teen number of customer owned and operated driverless trucks, with the deployment of trucks for Atlas likely to drive strong revenue growth in coming quarters.

Customers own the vehicles, which are operated by Kodiak as a service. Kodiak expects something like $150-300 thousand USD annual revenue per vehicle, dependent on assumptions and business model (per mile fee of license fee per vehicle). This likely means that revenue in 2026 will be in the high single digit or low double-digit million dollar range.

Kodiak's GAAP operating loss in Q3 was $30 million USD, while its non-GAAP operating loss was $24.7 million USD and free cash flow was negative $40 million USD. Kodiak ended Q3 with $146.2 million USD in cash and cash equivalents, meaning it will need to raise addition capital in the next 1-2 years. Given the company's current market capitalization, the dilution necessary to finance the business through to breakeven is likely to be material.

Conclusion

Kodiak AI recently went public via a SPAC, but it is yet to really capture the imagine of investors. This isn't really surprising, as the self-driving space has largely been shunned by the market, excluding Tesla.

Kodiak still needs to demonstrate fully driverless capabilities on public roads, and additional capital will be needed to finance the business through to breakeven. If this can be achieved, Kodiak could generate strong returns for investors. For example, analysts are currently projecting around $2.2 billion USD revenue in 2034, against a current market capitalization of approximately $1.7 billion USD.

I believe that it is difficult to predict the likely future market structure and competitive landscape though, making it difficult to have confidence in any particular company. In addition, Kodiak's revenue won't really begin to scale until at least 2027/2028, leaving investors exposed to significant downside in the meantime.

While the deployment of fully driverless vehicles on longer routes could be a catalyst in 2026, it should be kept in mind that this event did little for Aurora's share price. There is a significant valuation discrepancy between the two companies though, and this is likely to close somewhat if and when Kodiak commences commercial long-haul operations.

data.title: SCHD: Modeling Expected Changes For The March 2026 Reconstitution
data.description:
Introduction

This article previews possible upcoming changes to the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), whose Index is set to reconstitute effective March 23, 2026. Using the Index's published methodology, I model a hypothetical post-rebalance portfolio and present its aggregate fundamentals and sector mix, which I hope will assist readers in deciding whether it's worth waiting until March to buy or if they should consider planning an exit strategy. I've assigned SCHD a neutral "hold" rating to reflect this uncertainty, but I am optimistic about these forecasted changes, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

SCHD Overview

SCHD tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index, selecting stocks that have paid dividends for at least ten straight years and whose market caps are above $500 million. REITs are not included, nor are the remaining stocks that rank in the bottom 50% by indicated dividend yield. The Index selects the top 100 stocks based on a composite scoring system that combines dividend yield, dividend growth, return on equity, and free cash flow-to-debt, while allowing current components that rank within the top 200 to remain. Lastly, the Index weights are based on float-adjusted market cap with a 4% security and 25% sector cap, with annual reconstitutions effective after the third Friday in March.

SCHD investors appreciate its excellent mix of dividend yield and dividend growth. Along with VYM, VIG, and DGRO, SCHD is one of four large-cap value ETFs on a 10+ year dividend growth streak. When combined with its 3.77% estimated dividend yield, 9.15% five-year annualized dividend growth rate, 0.06% expense ratio, and $73.0B in net assets, it's easy to see its appeal. At the same time, critics point out that SCHD's 3Y and 5Y total returns through December 2025 were relatively soft, assessing that its defensive tilt and focus on dividends are not worth the sacrifice in capital appreciation potential.

SCHD Forecasted Sector-Level Changes

The model I built to forecast possible changes to SCHD's portfolio in March closely follows its Index's published methodology, though it's not perfect. My data source reports certain fundamental statistics slightly differently, and dividend yields won't be finalized until the end of February, leaving room for a few borderline stocks like IBM (IBM) and Cisco Systems (CSCO) to qualify. However, even with these limitations, I'm confident SCHD's Energy sector exposure will be meaningfully reduced by about 8%. Here's what I expect from a sector allocations perspective:

I anticipate Halliburton (HAL) will slightly miss the median yield screen, while Valero Energy (VLO) and Ovintiv (OVV) should exit due to large annual declines in ROE. Other Energy stocks like SLB N.V. (SLB) and ConocoPhillips (COP) will see their allocations reduced to align with their current market caps.

I also expect Financials to receive a 5% boost in total exposure, led primarily by the addition of Blackstone Group (BX). Other possible adds are CME Group (CME) and The Progressive Corp. (PGR).

SCHD Changes: ROE And Dividend Growth Are The Statistics That Matter

As I mentioned earlier, many investors buy SCHD for its excellent mix of dividend yield and dividend growth. However, few analysts closely examine the Index's four fundamental screens, and even fewer realize the sensitivity of each input into the composite scoring system. Having closely replicated the ranking model, I can confidently say that ROE and dividend growth are the key statistics that will matter in March.

To illustrate, consider the following table that lists these four statistics for the top 25 stocks I forecast SCHD to be underweight in March relative to its current portfolio. I've highlighted Valero Energy (VLO) as a nice example - its 5.24% ROE is far below the current portfolio's 27.57% average, and down from the 10.73% ROE I reported last March. In total, these stocks have a 21.96% weighted average ROE, which is about 6% below the current average and 8% below the forecasted average of 29.65%.

That's not to say the other statistics don't matter. The table also reveals that these underweight positions yield 3.50% compared to the current 3.83% average. In addition, they've collectively increased their dividends per share by 7.96%, which is 0.60% below the current average. However, these impacts are smaller than ROE's impact. Notably, there's hardly any variance in the debt as a percentage of free cash flow figures.

In contrast, dividend growth is the driving factor when it comes to SCHD's forecasted additions. As shown below, the top 25 overweighted positions have increased their dividends by an annualized 11.23% over the last five years in aggregate, 2.67% more than the current average. In particular, Blackstone Group's (BX) 16.69% 5Y Dividend CAGR benefits from a favorable base year of 2020, when its dividend growth was flat after declining by 21% the year prior.

We also see these positions yield 3.90%, which is a slight improvement over the Index's current 3.83% figure. ROE is essentially a wash (27.18% VS. 27.57%), while these overweight positions marginally reduced the fund's debt as a percentage of free cash flow.

Quick Summary: On March 23, 2026, expect SCHD to delete companies whose profits, as measured by ROE, have materially declined over the past year, and increase exposure to companies with strong dividend growth track records and marginally better yield and debt ratios.

A Brief Word About ROE

ROE is structurally a more sensitive capital efficiency ratio than ROTC (return on capital), which may lead SCHD to experience significant year-over-year changes despite no changes to a company's underlying business strength and dividend fundamentals. The reason is twofold:

1. ROE has a smaller denominator than ROTC (equity vs. debt + equity). Ratios with smaller denominators are more sensitive to absolute changes in the numerator.
